hook: The oldest surviving cantina in the Centro Histórico, pouring since 1869
facts: Opened in 1869, originally named La Oriental, then Habana, and renamed Tío Pepe in 1912.
Located at Av. Independencia 26, corner of Dolores, one block from the Palacio de Bellas Artes.
Retains its original interior: a long wooden bar, table service bells used to summon waiters, and the etched-glass sign above the bar.
For most of its history it barred women from entry, a rule only dropped in recent decades.
Beat writer William S. Burroughs, who lived in Mexico City from 1949, references the cantina in anecdotes from his book Junkie.
legend: Said to have been frequented by President Porfirio Díaz, who liked to drink in the centro.

sources.note: History: opened 1869, name changes, original features, Porfirio Díaz
History of the no-women rule and address Av. Independencia 26
Coordinates for Independencia 26, Centro (19.4331, -99.1430)

w: The oldest cantina still standing in the Centro, open since 1869 — the long wooden bar, the etched-glass sign, and little bells on the tables to ring for a waiter, all original. Women weren't let in until quite recently. Burroughs name-checked it in Junkie, and the story goes Porfirio Díaz drank here too. Ring for a tequila.

hook: A century-old pulquería reborn as a youth cult shrine to pulque and curados
facts: Opened in October 1902 at Aranda 28, just off Ayuntamiento in the Centro Histórico.
Has had only four owners; current owner Arturo Garrido rescued and revived it around 1998.
Walls were painted floor-to-ceiling with Mexica/pre-Hispanic motifs by artist Guillermo Carreño.
Serves pulque from Nanacamilpa, Tlaxcala, with a rotating menu of curados (flavored pulque) such as oat, celery and guava.
Known for reviving pulque culture among a young, mixed crowd in the 21st century.

sources.note: History: opened 1902, four owners, Garrido revival ~1998, Carreño murals
Curados from Nanacamilpa pulque, ambiance
Coordinates 19.4302465, -99.1427278 for Aranda 28

w: A 1902 pulquería off Ayuntamiento, walls painted floor to ceiling with Mexica gods, where a young crowd packs in for pulque and curados — oat, celery, guava — fermented agave straight from Tlaxcala. Arturo Garrido rescued the place around 1998 and made it the shrine of pulque's revival. Cloudy, sour, alive. Squeeze in.

hook: The smoky 1950s café where Castro and Che plotted and Bolaño's Infrarealists met
facts: Founded in the 1950s at Morelos 62, corner of Av. Bucareli, Colonia Juárez.
Opened by a Spaniard who had arrived in Mexico via Cuba, lending it its Havana name and old-Latin-American-café feel.
Fidel Castro and Ernesto 'Che' Guevara are reputed to have met and talked here while exiled in Mexico in the 1950s.
Between 1975 and 1977 it was a main gathering point of the Infrarealist poetry movement led by Roberto Bolaño and Mario Santiago Papasquiaro.
Bolaño alludes to the café in his novel Los detectives salvajes (The Savage Detectives).
legend: Gabriel García Márquez is said to have written passages of Cien años de soledad seated by its large windows.

sources.note: History, address Morelos 62 esq. Bucareli, coordinates 19.4324664/-99.1533563, Infrarealism, Bolaño, García Márquez
Mythic café history and intellectual clientele
Che Guevara and Fidel Castro visits

w: A smoky old Latin-American café on Bucareli, open since the 1950s, where Castro and Che are said to have met in exile. Two decades on it was the haunt of Bolaño's Infrarealist poets, and a setting in his Savage Detectives. García Márquez supposedly wrote here by the windows. A café con leche and a long afternoon.

hook: Mexico City's oldest dance hall: "Whoever doesn't know Los Ángeles doesn't know Mexico"
facts: Inaugurated 29 July 1937 with Gonzalo Curiel's orchestra and the Danzonera de Toto, in a former timber/coal warehouse in Colonia Guerrero (Lerdo 206).
Its 1948 motto, coined by owner Miguel Nieto, is 'Quien no conoce Los Ángeles, no conoce México.'
Specializes in danzón, mambo, son and salsa, with live big-band dancing on Tuesdays and Sundays.
Declared Intangible Cultural Heritage of Mexico City.
The 1991 María Novaro film Danzón and other films were shot on its dance floor.
legend: Said to have hosted dancers including Gabriel García Márquez, Carlos Fuentes, Carlos Monsiváis, Frida Kahlo and Diego Rivera.

sources.note: Founding 1937, Guerrero location, motto, heritage status, film history
Danzón history and cultural mythology
Coordinates 19.450503, -99.141899 for Lerdo 206, Guerrero

w: 'Whoever doesn't know Los Ángeles doesn't know Mexico' — the motto of the city's oldest dance hall, swinging since 1937 in a former coal warehouse in the Guerrero. Danzón, mambo, son and salsa, live big bands on Tuesdays and Sundays, declared cultural heritage. María Novaro shot Danzón on this floor. They say Frida and Diego danced here.

hook: The Cathedral of Lucha Libre, where masked wrestlers have flown since 1956
facts: The current arena was inaugurated 27 April 1956 by Salvador Lutteroth, founder of the EMLL (now Consejo Mundial de Lucha Libre, CMLL).
Built by architect Francisco Bullman on the site of the older Arena Modelo, at Calle Dr. Lavista 189, Colonia Doctores.
Seats roughly 16,500, making it the largest dedicated lucha libre venue in the world.
Nicknamed 'la Catedral de la Lucha Libre.'
At the 1956 inauguration, comic actor Cantinflas served as padrino, breaking a champagne bottle on a corner of the ring.
legend: The 1956 opening card is said to have featured the legends El Santo and Blue Demon.

sources.note: Address Calle Dr. Lavista 189 Doctores, capacity 16,500, opened 1956, coordinates 19.42472/-99.15194
History as Cathedral of Lucha Libre, Lutteroth, Cantinflas, Santo and Blue Demon

w: The Cathedral of Lucha Libre, 16,500 seats of it — the largest wrestling arena on earth, where masked luchadores have flown off the ropes since 1956. Salvador Lutteroth built it; Cantinflas smashed champagne on the ring at the opening, and El Santo and Blue Demon, the story goes, fought the first card. Buy a cheap seat and a mask.

hook: The witches' market: a maze of herbs, amulets, santería and limpias since 1957
facts: Inaugurated 23 September 1957 at Fray Servando Teresa de Mier 419, Colonia Merced Balbuena, alongside the Jamaica and La Merced markets.
Originally called Mercado Baños for the public baths and showers it once held; named for the nearby Cine Sonora.
Famous for its esoteric/herbolaria section: candles, saints' images, healing herbs, talismans and love potions.
A smaller hall ('nave menor') houses practitioners of brujería, healing limpias and fortune-telling.
Also long known, more controversially, as a market for exotic and live animals.
legend: Popularly called 'el mercado de los brujos' (the wizards' market) for its concentration of witchcraft sellers.

sources.note: History: 1957 opening, Mercado Baños/Cine Sonora origin, esoteric sections
64th anniversary, address Fray Servando 419, sections detail

w: The witches' market, trading since 1957 — a maze of stalls heaped with healing herbs, saints' candles, amulets, love potions and luck. In the back hall, brujos give limpias, cleansing you of bad energy, and fortune-tellers read what's coming. Locals just call it the mercado de los brujos. Bring an open mind, and small change.

hook: An Art Déco oval park laid over an old racetrack, the green heart of the Condesa
facts: Officially Parque San Martín, opened in 1927 on the former Jockey Club horse-racing track in Colonia Hipódromo.
Its oval shape preserves the outline of the old hipódromo, and the surrounding streets curve outward from it.
A landmark of Art Déco urban design, with the Foro Lindbergh amphitheatre at its centre.
The Fuente de los Cántaros, a sculpture of a woman carrying two water jugs by José María Fernández Urbina, stands in the park.
Around 9 hectares with ponds, fountains and tree-trunk-shaped lamps; a model of early 20th-century planned coexistence of buildings and nature.

sources.note: Officially Parque San Martín, Art Déco, Hipódromo Condesa, area, coordinates 19.41222/-99.16917
History, 1927 opening on former racetrack, Foro Lindbergh, Fuente de los Cántaros

w: The green heart of the Condesa, an oval Art Déco park laid out in 1927 over the old Jockey Club racetrack — the streets still curve to the shape of the vanished hipódromo. At its centre, the Foro Lindbergh amphitheatre and a fountain of a woman pouring from two jugs. Lamps like tree trunks, ponds, shade. The city's loveliest loop.

hook: A two-floor vinyl institution at the heart of Roma's record-shop scene
facts: Founded in 2012, an independent record store and label, with branches in Roma Norte and Coyoacán.
Located at Álvaro Obregón 200 Bis, near the corner of Insurgentes in Roma Norte.
Ground floor stocks new releases, reissues, Mexican pressings and used vinyl in 7", 10" and 12" formats.
Upstairs has a DJ-focused section with turntables, mixers, players and electronic-music singles.
A landmark of the Roma vinyl revival, repeatedly listed among Mexico City's top record shops.

sources.note: Time Out México profile: location, two floors, founding context
Official site: founded 2012, label, branches
Coordinates 19.4171670, -99.1642364 for Álvaro Obregón 200, Roma Norte

w: Two floors of vinyl on Álvaro Obregón, the anchor of Roma's record-shop revival since 2012 — new releases, reissues, Mexican pressings and second-hand crates downstairs, a DJ floor of turntables and electronic singles up top. It runs its own label, too, and lands on every best-of-the-city list. Dig downstairs, then go up and play.
